response: The internet traces its roots to U.S. Defense Department networking experiments of the 1960s, with ARPANET first transmitting a message between UCLA and Stanford in 1969 and migrating to the TCP/IP standard in 1983. Tim Berners-Lee’s creation of the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989—introducing HTML, URLs, and HTTP—followed by the Mosaic browser in 1993 opened the network to the public and fueled the late-1990s dot-com boom, whose 2001 crash left resilient giants such as Amazon and Google. The mid-2000s rise of Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, the advent of AWS cloud services in 2006, and the iPhone-driven shift to mobile internet in 2007 further reshaped online life. Today, the internet links more than 5 billion people and underpins virtually every sphere of global commerce, communication, and entertainment.
